howItWorks.intro: This calculator compares the complete financial picture of entering a skilled trade via apprenticeship versus attending a 4-year college, accounting for earnings during training, debt, and career trajectory.
rules: Trade path: 4-5 year apprenticeship earning $15-25/hr while training, then journeyman wages of $50-80K depending on trade and location
College path: 4 years of tuition with no significant earnings, followed by degree-appropriate starting salary with annual growth
Cumulative earnings include all money earned minus education costs and student loan payments
Trade growth assumes gradual increases plus potential for self-employment/contracting at higher rates
howItWorks.outro: Financial outcome is just one factor. Consider job satisfaction, physical demands, career flexibility, and long-term health impact. Many trades offer excellent financial outcomes with the advantage of earning from day one.

faq.question: Do tradespeople really earn more than college graduates?
faq.answer: In the first 10-15 years, many tradespeople have higher cumulative earnings than college graduates because they start earning immediately and have no student debt. Over 20+ years, college graduates in high-paying fields (engineering, CS, healthcare) often catch up and surpass. For lower-paying majors (education, arts), trades may maintain the advantage permanently.

faq.question: How much does a trade apprenticeship cost?
faq.answer: Most trade apprenticeships cost very little — often $1,000-5,000 for tools and community college classes. Many union apprenticeships are fully funded by the employer. Compare this to $88,000-208,000 for a 4-year college degree. Apprentices also earn $15-25/hour while training.

faq.question: What are the highest-paying trade careers?
faq.answer: Top-paying trades include: elevator installers ($88K median), electrical power-line workers ($82K), boilermakers ($65K), plumbers ($60K), electricians ($60K), and HVAC technicians ($52K). Self-employed/master tradespeople often earn $80-120K+. Union trades in high cost-of-living areas can exceed $100K with benefits.

faq.question: What are the downsides of a trade career?
faq.answer: Physical demands are the biggest concern — trade careers can be hard on the body, with higher injury rates. Work may be seasonal or project-based. Career ceiling is lower without transitioning to management or self-employment. Physical ability typically declines with age, potentially limiting earning years compared to office-based careers.

faq.question: Can I switch from a trade to a different career later?
faq.answer: Yes, but it's harder without a degree. Many tradespeople transition to inspection, project management, sales, or teaching. Some start their own contracting businesses. A growing number of companies value trade experience for management roles. You can also attend college later with savings from your trade career — debt-free.

faq.question: Is there a shortage of tradespeople?
faq.answer: Yes, significant. The skilled trades face a massive labor shortage, with an estimated 650,000 unfilled construction jobs alone in 2025. This shortage is driving up wages and creating excellent job security. The average tradesperson is over 55, meaning retirements will intensify the shortage for decades.
